data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The administrative apparatus of the Late Bronze Age palace societies reached a complexity threshold that generated feedback loops of elite competition, fiscal overextension, and loss of bureaucratic cohesion.","mechanism": "Increasingly layered hierarchies amplified coordination costs, leading to rapid institutional decay once external shocks reduced surplus resources.","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece","Hittite Anatolia"],"excludes": ["Ugarit","Levantine city‑states","Egypt"]},"forbidden": [{"case": "Inscriptions from Hattusa during the 12th c BCE demonstrate a streamlined, low‑overhead administration", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes": "A systems‑theoretic model that attributes collapse to internal network dynamics, independent of climate or external invasion."}
